title: Simple Interest Calculator 2026 — Formula I = P × r × t | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Simple Interest: Formula I = P × r × t
description: Simple interest calculator: compute interest and final amount in seconds. Loans, short-term investments, late fees. With examples and comparison.
seoKeywords: I = P x r x t
intro: **Simple interest** is the most basic way to calculate return or cost on capital: it's always applied to the **original principal**, without reinvesting earned interest. The formula is **I = P × r × t** (interest = principal × rate × time) and is used for **late payment interest**, **short-term personal loans**, **discount Treasury bills**, and as a quick approximation when there's no compounding. This calculator asks for **principal**, **annual rate**, and **time** (in years, months, or days) and returns the **final amount**, **total interest**, and **average monthly and daily interest**. For long-term investments with reinvested interest, use the compound interest calculator.
keyTakeaway: **Simple interest = Principal × Rate × Time**. Interest **is NOT reinvested** (unlike compound). Used for **late fees, short loans**, and as an approximation. Over long periods, compound yields much more.
useCases: You're calculating late payment interest on a bill, rent, or invoice.
You want to estimate how much you'll pay on a 6-month personal loan with a fixed rate.
You're comparing simple vs. compound interest to decide on an investment.
You're settling interest on a tax debt.
You're drafting a private agreement and need the amount updated to a specific date.

example.title: Example: $100,000 at 10% annual for 2 years
example.result: With a 10% simple annual rate on $100,000 over 2 years, you earn **$20,000 in interest** and end with **$120,000**. If it had been compounded, you'd end with $121,000 — the gap widens with time.

explanation:
## What Is Simple Interest

Simple interest is the oldest calculation model: interest is **always computed on the original principal**, never on prior interest. There's no compounding.

```
I = P × r × t
Final amount = P × (1 + r × t)
```

Where:
- **I** = total interest
- **P** = initial principal
- **r** = interest rate as decimal (10% → 0.10)
- **t** = time in the same unit as the rate

## Time in the Same Unit as the Rate

| Rate | Time in | Conversion |
|---|---|---|
| **Annual** (10%) | Years | t = years |
| **Annual** (10%) | Months | t = months / 12 |
| **Annual** (10%) | Days | t = days / 365 |
| **Monthly** (1%) | Months | t = months |
| **Daily** | Days | t = days |

## Simple vs. Compound — The Gap Grows

Over short periods the difference is minimal. Long term, compound crushes simple:

| Period | Simple (r=10% annual) | Compound (r=10% annual) | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Start | $1,000 | $1,000 | — |
| Year 1 | $1,100 | $1,100 | 0% |
| Year 2 | $1,200 | $1,210 | +0.8% |
| Year 5 | $1,500 | $1,611 | +7.4% |
| Year 10 | $2,000 | $2,594 | +29.7% |
| Year 20 | $3,000 | $6,727 | +124% |
| Year 30 | $4,000 | $17,449 | +336% |

Einstein called compound interest 'the eighth wonder of the world.' Use compound interest calculations for long-term comparisons.

## Typical Simple Interest Use Cases

### 1. Late Payment Interest

Contracts often specify a **daily simple punitive rate** between 0.1% and 0.3%. For a $5,000 debt 30 days late at 0.2%:

```
interest = 5,000 × 0.002 × 30 = $300
```

### 2. Short-Term Personal Loans

Some lenders apply simple interest on 3-12 month loans. Real effective cost (APR) is always higher due to fees.

### 3. Discount Treasury Bills

T-bills trade at a discount: you pay less than face value today and receive 100% at maturity. Return is often expressed as a simple annual rate.

## Why Banks Show APR but Charge APY

**APR (Annual Percentage Rate)** is the nominal reference rate, without compounding. **APY (Annual Percentage Yield)** is what you actually pay or earn when compounding exists. With APR 12% and monthly compounding:

```
APY = (1 + 0.12/12)^12 − 1 ≈ 12.68%
```

In credit cards, where compounding is daily, the effect is even worse.

## Common Mistakes When Calculating Simple Interest

1. **Not converting units**: if the rate is annual and time is in months, forgetting to divide by 12 triples the result.
2. **Using 360 vs. 365 days**: many US contracts use 360 for business. Confirm with your contract.
3. **Mixing simple with compound**: if the period is longer than 1 year, it's rarely real simple interest.
4. **Not reading the APR**: the rate alone doesn't tell you the real cost when there are fees.

faq.q: What's the difference between simple and compound interest?
faq.a: In **simple interest**, interest is **always computed on the original principal**. In **compound**, it's computed on principal + accumulated interest. Short term, they're nearly identical, but **over 10 years a 10% compound rate yields 30% more than simple**. For investing, always use compound.

It's used for **late payment penalties**, **late rent fees**, **short personal loans** (under 12 months), **discount Treasury bills**, and some **private contracts**. For CDs and mortgages, compound interest is used.

faq.q: How do I convert an annual rate to monthly in simple interest?
faq.a: Simply **divide**: monthly rate = **APR / 12**. Example: APR 36% → 3% simple monthly. **Note**: this does NOT work for compound, where you use `(1 + APR)^(1/12) − 1`. Compounded, APR 36% equals ~2.6% monthly.

faq.q: Can I use simple interest to calculate my CD return?
faq.a: **Yes, traditional CDs are calculated with simple interest** prorated by days: `interest = principal × APR/100 × days/365`. A 30-day CD at 5% APR yields 0.41% of the period. **However**, if you enable **automatic rollover**, interest compounds and you start earning compound — that's the APY.

faq.q: What should I use to compare loan offers?
faq.a: Compare by **APR (Annual Percentage Rate)** which already includes fees and insurance. The simple nominal rate can be misleading. Two loans with the same nominal rate but different fees have very different real costs.

faq.q: I paid a debt late, how do I calculate penalties?
faq.a: Depends on the contract. If it says '**X% monthly late fee**,' it's simple: `debt × X/100 × months`. If it says '**prime rate + Y%**,' look up the prime rate + Y and apply simple interest over the days.

faq.q: Is simple interest good for investing?
faq.a: **No, never use simple interest for long-term investing**. The difference vs. compound is astronomical over decades. Simple interest is mostly used for short-term instruments or as an approximation.

**No**, it calculates gross amounts. Tax treatment varies widely by country. In the US, interest income is generally taxed at your ordinary income rate. Subtract 15-25% from interest earned depending on your tax bracket.

howToSteps.name: Click Calculate
